narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 53% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is just over the threshold we use to approve admissions. This prediction is based on your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.0, an LSAT score of 29, and your placement in the bottom decile for grades in both your first and third years of school—areas where you fall well below the typical student's performance. Your middle family income, status as a male Hispanic student planning full-time enrollment, also factored in, though these align more closely with common profiles in our dataset. The model's decision was driven most strongly by your LSAT score, which had the biggest influence in pushing the prediction toward failure compared to what it sees in stronger applicants. Right behind that was your third-year decile ranking, followed closely by your first-year decile, both signaling early and persistent academic struggles relative to peers who typically rank in the middle of their classes. Your family income level played a moderate role in the same direction, as did your racial background, while your undergraduate GPA, gender, and full-time plans had smaller but still contributing influences toward the failure prediction. None of these features pulled strongly in favor of passing, which is why the overall outlook tipped negative. Compared to the average applicant—who scores around 36.7 on the LSAT, has a 3.21 GPA, and middle-of-the-pack school rankings—your metrics in the key academic areas stand out as notably lower, making them the dominant factors here. To improve your future chances, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT to aim for a score closer to or above the average, strengthen your academic performance through targeted study habits or additional coursework to boost future grade deciles, and build a stronger undergraduate record if possible before reapplying. These steps could shift the model's view significantly, as they address the top-ranked influences. With dedication, students in similar situations have turned things around—keep pursuing your goal.
